segments.text: During World War II, the US Navy noticed
a mysterious shadow on their sonar. It
was too shallow to be the seafloor, but
more alarmingly, it moved. Every night,
it would creep closer to the ship only
to slink away again during the day. Was
it a hidden fleet? A moving continent, a
giant monster? To find out, we have to
dive deep. This zone of the ocean is
called the twilight zone. Light
struggles to reach down here, and the
food is scarce. So every night some
animals rise from this deep dark place,
swim hundreds of meters to the surface
where they feed safely under the cover
of night and then they return to the
dark during the daytime to hide. A few
animals doing this wouldn't show up like
this on the sonar, but billions of tons
of them would. These sailors were
unknowingly witnessing the world's
largest migration. A global event so big
that it churns the nutrients of our
oceans and it protects us by quickly
pulling harmful carbon back deep into
the sea. But today, there are still a
lot of questions about what exactly is
happening here. to learn about how
they're going to find out more.
